title: Dietary Pattern Self-Assessment
description: Evaluate how your current eating patterns align with evidence-based dietary approaches that support mental health. Based on Mediterranean Diet Adherence and ultra-processed food research.
instructions: Think about your typical eating habits over the past month. How often do you do the following?

questions.text: Eat fruits and vegetables (at least 5 servings per day)
Include whole grains (oats, brown rice, whole wheat bread)
Eat fatty fish (salmon, sardines, mackerel) at least twice per week
Use olive oil as your primary cooking fat
Include legumes (beans, lentils, chickpeas) in your meals
Eat nuts or seeds as snacks or in meals
Consume ultra-processed foods (packaged snacks, fast food, sugary drinks)
Eat fermented foods (yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, kimchi)
Cook meals at home using whole, unprocessed ingredients
Eat a wide variety of different plant foods each week
Consume foods high in added sugar (desserts, candy, sweetened cereals)

bands.label: Room for Improvement
bands.description: Your current dietary pattern may not be providing optimal support for your mental health. Research shows that shifting toward a Mediterranean-style pattern can reduce depression risk by up to 32% (SMILES Trial). This course will show you practical, gradual changes you can make.

bands.label: Building Foundations
bands.description: You have some healthy eating habits in place. Studies show even moderate Mediterranean diet adherence is associated with lower anxiety and stress. This course will help you identify which specific changes could have the biggest impact on your mood.

bands.label: Good Dietary Foundation
bands.description: Your dietary pattern shows many elements that research links to better mental health outcomes. You're already incorporating anti-inflammatory foods and limiting some processed items. This course will help you fine-tune your approach with the latest evidence.

bands.label: Strong Dietary Pattern
bands.description: Your eating habits closely align with dietary patterns shown to support mental health. Research from the BMJ shows these patterns are associated with 50% lower risk of common mental disorders. This course will deepen your understanding of why your habits work.

disclaimer: This is an educational self-reflection tool, not a clinical dietary assessment. Note: Questions 7, 8, and 12 ask about habits that research associates with increased mental health risk — scoring higher on these items is not beneficial. For a comprehensive dietary evaluation, consult a registered dietitian. If you have an eating disorder, please discuss any dietary changes with your treatment team. If you are in crisis, call or text 988.
